rareDiseaseScoring.title: Rare Disease Scoring
rareDiseaseScoring.heroSub: The mathematical engine behind {brandName}. We use Bayesian probabilities and semantic similarity to rank thousands of rare diseases against your patient's exact phenotype.
rareDiseaseScoring.overviewP1: The fundamental flaw in legacy clinical decision support systems and basic EHR search functionalities is their reliance on rigid, boolean keyword matching. In these archaic systems, if a physician searches for a disease using the term "Macrocephaly," the system will strictly return diseases where the exact string "Macrocephaly" is documented in the database profile. If a disease profile instead lists "Abnormally large head circumference," a basic keyword search will fail to make the connection, entirely missing a potentially life-saving diagnosis. This lack of semantic intelligence is unacceptable in the nuanced field of rare disease genetics. The {brandName} Rare Disease Scoring engine represents a quantum leap forward by abandoning rigid keywords in favor of mathematically rigorous Semantic Similarity Scoring. Because {brandName}’s entire architecture is built upon the directed acyclic graph of the Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO), the scoring algorithm understands the biological relationships between symptoms. It knows that "Arachnodactyly" is a highly specific child term of the broader "Abnormality of the digits." When calculating a differential diagnosis, the algorithm compares the physician-approved list of patient HPO terms against the known phenotypic profiles of over 11,000 rare diseases stored in the Orphanet database. It computes a sophisticated similarity matrix, awarding high mathematical scores for exact matches, partial scores for fuzzy hierarchical matches (e.g., the patient has the broad symptom, but the disease specifically features the narrower child symptom), and heavily penalizing the score when the patient exhibits a major phenotype that is completely antithetical to the suspected disease's known presentation.
rareDiseaseScoring.f1Desc: The fundamental flaw in legacy clinical decision support systems and basic EHR search functionalities is their reliance on rigid, boolean keyword matching. In these archaic systems, if a physician searches for a disease using the term "Macrocephaly," the system will strictly return diseases where the exact string "Macrocephaly" is documented in the database profile. If a disease profile instead lists "Abnormally large head circumference," a basic keyword search will fail to make the connection, entirely missing a potentially life-saving diagnosis. This lack of semantic intelligence is unacceptable in the nuanced field of rare disease genetics.
rareDiseaseScoring.f2Desc: A scoring algorithm that treats every symptom with equal mathematical weight will invariably produce useless results. A patient presenting with chronic fatigue, mild headaches, and severe, early-onset aortic root dilation has three distinct symptoms. However, any clinical geneticist knows intuitively that the aortic root dilation is the defining diagnostic clue, while the fatigue and headaches are merely background noise that could be attributed to stress, poor sleep, or hundreds of benign conditions.
rareDiseaseScoring.f3Title: Applying Bayesian Priors and Epidemiological Context
rareDiseaseScoring.f3Desc: While phenotypic matching is the core of the algorithm, a purely symptom-based approach ignores the reality of epidemiology. In clinical medicine, the famous adage "when you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras" speaks to the importance of statistical probability. If a patient's physical symptoms perfectly match two different genetic disorders—one with a global prevalence of 1 in 10,000, and another that has only been documented in three families in recorded medical history—it is statistically imperative that the physician considers the more common disorder first, without entirely ruling out the ultra-rare alternative.

clinvarPage.heroSubtitle: {brandName} seamlessly extracts variant mentions from standard clinical PDFs to provide instant clinical significance context.
clinvarPage.overviewText: As commercial genetic testing becomes increasingly accessible, the sheer volume of data generated by multi-gene panels and exome sequencing has exploded. While modern laboratory techniques excel at identifying molecular deviations from the reference genome, determining the actual clinical consequence of those deviations remains one of the most profound challenges in modern medicine. A sequence report may identify a dozen genetic mutations, but identifying a mutation is not a diagnosis; establishing pathogenicity is the diagnosis. This is where the public archive known as ClinVar becomes an indispensable tool for the clinical geneticist. Maintained by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) at the National Institutes of Health (NIH), ClinVar is a globally centralized database that aggregates submissions from commercial testing laboratories, academic research institutions, and specialized clinical clinics regarding the relationship between human genetic variations and observable health statuses. It tracks whether a specific variant (e.g., a particular missense mutation in the MYBPC3 gene) has been historically classified as Pathogenic, Benign, or, as is often the case, a Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS). Without access to this aggregated historical context, interpreting a modern genetic report is essentially impossible. However, manually cross-referencing a lengthy PDF genetic report against the ClinVar database is a tedious, error-prone, and time-consuming process. A clinician must manually type complex alphanumeric variant nomenclatures into a web portal, risking transcription errors that could pull up the clinical history for an entirely different genetic mutation. {brandName} automates this critical verification step entirely, integrating the vast wealth of ClinVar data directly into the physician's unified workspace.
clinvarPage.feat1Desc: As commercial genetic testing becomes increasingly accessible, the sheer volume of data generated by multi-gene panels and exome sequencing has exploded. While modern laboratory techniques excel at identifying molecular deviations from the reference genome, determining the actual clinical consequence of those deviations remains one of the most profound challenges in modern medicine. A sequence report may identify a dozen genetic mutations, but identifying a mutation is not a diagnosis; establishing pathogenicity is the diagnosis. This is where the public archive known as ClinVar becomes an indispensable tool for the clinical geneticist.
clinvarPage.feat2Desc: {brandName}’s integration with ClinVar is built upon our advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) architecture. When a physician or patient uploads a standard commercial genetic testing PDF report, {brandName}’s engine scans the document specifically searching for standardized genetic nomenclature and gene symbols. It identifies the targeted gene, the specific molecular alteration, and the classification provided by the issuing laboratory at the time the report was printed.
clinvarPage.feat3Desc: The dynamic nature of genetic classification creates a significant logistical nightmare for busy genetics clinics. When a patient is discharged with an unresolved VUS, the clinic bears a moral, and increasingly legal, responsibility to inform the patient if that variant is ever reclassified to Pathogenic in the future. However, actively monitoring thousands of historic patient files for potential ClinVar updates is an administrative impossibility for human staff.

doctorWorkspace.heroSubtitle: Your centralized command center. Manage patient intakes, review AI-extracted evidence, and orchestrate the diagnostic journey all from one secure portal.
doctorWorkspace.s1P1: A specialist at a high-volume tertiary care center or a dedicated rare disease clinic does not simply see one patient at a time. They manage an overwhelming, continuously shifting queue of complex cases. Every day, faxes arrive, physical binders of printed medical records are mailed in, and digital PDFs are dumped into legacy EHR portals. The cognitive load required simply to organize these documents—let alone synthesize them into a coherent clinical picture—is immense. This administrative burden directly contributes to physician burnout and drastically slows the speed at which acute patients receive critical diagnoses.
doctorWorkspace.s1P2: The {brandName} Doctor Workspace was engineered explicitly to solve this intake and triage bottleneck. It serves as a unified, highly intelligent command center. Instead of staring at an undifferentiated list of names in an EHR, a physician logging into {brandName} is presented with a dynamically prioritized queue. When a new patient submits their pre-intake forms and uploads their records, the system does not passively store the PDFs. Our NLP engines immediately process the documents, extracting the severity of the clinical phenotypes. The workspace then automatically pushes highly acute cases—for example, a pediatric patient exhibiting rapidly progressing neurological regression—to the very top of your queue, ensuring that critical care is never delayed by administrative backlog.
doctorWorkspace.s2P1: Once a physician selects a patient from the queue, they enter the individual Case Dashboard. This is the core operational theater of {brandName}. We have designed the interface to fundamentally eliminate "chart fragmentation." In a traditional workflow, a doctor might have an imaging report open on one monitor, a laboratory assay on another, and a word processor open to draft their notes. In the {brandName} Case Dashboard, everything is synthesized into a single pane of glass.
doctorWorkspace.s2P2: The left side of the workspace acts as your document viewer, allowing you to scroll through original, unmodified source documents (such as clinical notes, scanned letters, and lab PDFs). The right side of the workspace is the structured domain. Here, you see the exact, normalized Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO) terms that the AI has extracted from the chaotic text on the left. The true power of the workspace lies in its bi-directional mapping. If you click on the HPO term "Ectopia lentis" on the right, the document viewer on the left instantly snaps to page 47 of the uploaded binder and highlights the exact sentence where the referring ophthalmologist noted a dislocated lens. This traceability builds immense trust between the physician and the AI.
doctorWorkspace.s3P1: Diagnosing a multi-systemic rare syndrome is rarely achieved by a single specialist in isolation. It requires the collective intelligence of a Multidisciplinary Team (MDT)—neurologists, cardiologists, dysmorphologists, and genetic counselors working in concert. Historically, convening an MDT required scheduling a physical tumor board or a synchronous Zoom call, which is notoriously difficult given the busy schedules of attending physicians.
doctorWorkspace.s3P2: The {brandName} Workspace introduces secure, asynchronous collaboration. If you, as the primary geneticist, are reviewing the case and encounter a highly specific echocardiogram report, you do not need to pause your review and wait for a meeting. Directly within the workspace, you can tag a pediatric cardiologist colleague. They receive a secure alert, log into the workspace, and are routed only to the cardiac-specific extracted terms and the corresponding echo report. They can confirm the finding, leave a clinical note attached to the specific HPO term, and sign off. The central diagnostic algorithm recalculates the patient's rare disease scores in real-time as these asynchronous approvals roll in.
doctorWorkspace.s3P3: This collaborative model fundamentally accelerates the diagnostic timeline. A patient’s case never stalls simply because a team couldn't find an hour to meet in the same room. The workflow is entirely fluid, rigorously documented, and fully auditable, allowing the world’s best specialists to collaborate seamlessly across different departments, or even different continents.

clinicalNotes.heroSubtitle: Unlock the hidden data in unstructured text. {brandName}'s proprietary clinical NLP engines read through years of specialist notes to extract the full patient story.
clinicalNotes.overviewText: The vast majority of the world's actionable clinical knowledge does not exist in neatly organized, easily queried databases. Instead, an estimated 80% of all medical data is trapped within the chaotic, unstructured text of clinical notes. When a patient with a rare, undiagnosed condition finally secures an appointment at a tertiary care center, they rarely arrive with a simple, structured summary. They arrive with a "data dump"—often consisting of hundreds of pages of discharge summaries, progress notes from various unconnected specialists, hastily typed referral letters, and sometimes even decades-old handwritten records that have been scanned into degraded PDFs. For a clinical geneticist, finding the single diagnostic clue hidden within this mountain of text is akin to searching for a needle in a haystack while blindfolded. A subtle observation noted by a pediatric ophthalmologist five years ago—perhaps a passing mention of a slightly dislocated lens—might be the definitive phenotypic link required to diagnose Marfan Syndrome today. Yet, if that note is buried on page 142 of a 300-page PDF, the human physician is highly likely to miss it during a standard 45-minute consultation. The sheer volume of unstructured data acts as a massive operational bottleneck, directly contributing to the multi-year diagnostic odysseys experienced by millions of rare disease patients. {brandName} was fundamentally engineered to solve this unstructured data crisis. We recognize that doctors went to medical school to solve complex biological puzzles, not to act as administrative data-miners. By deploying medical-grade artificial intelligence to pre-read and synthesize these vast archives of unstructured text, {brandName} liberates the physician to focus entirely on the intellectual task of diagnosis and patient care.
clinicalNotes.feat1Desc: The vast majority of the world's actionable clinical knowledge does not exist in neatly organized, easily queried databases. Instead, an estimated 80% of all medical data is trapped within the chaotic, unstructured text of clinical notes.
clinicalNotes.feat2Title: Advanced NLP Entity Extraction and Contextual Reasoning
clinicalNotes.feat2Desc: Parsing medical text requires significantly more sophistication than standard keyword searching. {brandName} utilizes proprietary Clinical Natural Language Processing (NLP) models that possess a deep semantic understanding of medical vernacular, syntax, and clinical context.
clinicalNotes.feat3Desc: Extracting symptoms in isolation is only half the battle; understanding how those symptoms evolved over time is critical for diagnosing complex syndromes. Many rare genetic diseases are characterized not just by the presence of certain phenotypes, but by the specific chronological order in which they appear.

clinicalPhotos.heroSubtitle: Many rare genetic disorders exhibit characteristic facial dysmorphism or dermatological manifestations. {brandName} securely incorporates visual evidence into the diagnostic process.
clinicalPhotos.overviewText: In the highly specialized field of clinical genetics, text-based medical records only tell a fraction of the patient's story. A significant percentage of rare genetic syndromes are characterized by unique physical traits, particularly craniofacial abnormalities and distinct dermatological manifestations. For decades, expert geneticists—known as dysmorphologists—have relied on a concept known as "gestalt," which is the intuitive, almost instantaneous recognition of a specific syndrome based entirely on the overall visual appearance of the patient's face and body. However, this incredible diagnostic skill is exceedingly rare. General practitioners, pediatricians, and even many sub-specialists simply do not possess the extensive mental library of rare disease phenotypes required to make these visual connections. If a patient presenting with subtle syndromic features lives hundreds of miles away from a major academic medical center, their visual diagnosis is often delayed for years. Textual descriptions of physical traits in clinical notes (e.g., "patient has low-set ears" or "mild micrognathia") are often highly subjective, poorly standardized, and insufficient for a definitive diagnosis. {brandName} bridges this gap by elevating high-resolution clinical photography to the same level of importance as textual clinical notes and laboratory values. By allowing referring physicians, or even the patients themselves via the pre-intake portal, to securely upload comprehensive sets of clinical photographs, {brandName} ensures that the vital visual context of the patient's condition is captured immediately. These images travel with the patient's digital file, ensuring that when the case finally reaches an expert dysmorphologist, they have the exact visual evidence required to apply their clinical gestalt, regardless of geographic barriers.
clinicalPhotos.feat1Title: The Power of Dysmorphology and Clinical "Gestalt"
clinicalPhotos.feat1Desc: In the highly specialized field of clinical genetics, text-based medical records only tell a fraction of the patient's story. expert geneticists relied on a concept known as "gestalt," the intuitive recognition of a syndrome based entirely on the overall visual appearance.
clinicalPhotos.feat2Desc: Within the {brandName} platform, the upload process is highly structured. Users are guided to categorize images based on standard anatomical regions and manifestation types.
clinicalPhotos.feat3Desc: Clinical photographs are the most identifiable form of PHI. {brandName} recognizes the profound sensitivity of this data and implements draconian controls to protect patient privacy.

labReports.heroSubtitle: Laboratory values are the objective truth in a clinical workup. {brandName} converts complex, multi-page laboratory PDFs into structured, actionable data streams.
labReports.overviewText: In the diagnostic pursuit of rare genetic and metabolic disorders, subjective clinical notes must invariably be corroborated by objective laboratory data. A physician’s observation of muscle weakness is crucial, but a serum creatine kinase level of 10,000 U/L is an undeniable, mathematically precise biological truth. However, the mechanism by which this critical laboratory data is transferred between disconnected healthcare systems remains archaic. When a patient arrives at a specialist clinic, their historical lab results are rarely transmitted as structured, interoperable data packets (like FHIR or HL7 v2 messages). Instead, they almost always arrive as flattened, multi-page PDF documents or degraded paper faxes. For standard Natural Language Processing (NLP) engines, extracting information from these flattened laboratory PDFs is notoriously difficult. Standard text-extraction algorithms read left-to-right, top-to-bottom, completely destroying the visual, columnar layout of a lab report table. If a PDF contains an analyte name in column one, the observed value in column two, and the reference range in column three, a rudimentary text parser will scramble these figures into an incomprehensible paragraph, completely severing the link between the biological marker and its corresponding quantitative result. {brandName} specifically engineered a proprietary Table Parsing Engine to overcome this precise infrastructural failure. When a laboratory PDF is uploaded into the system, our advanced computer vision algorithms do not merely read the text; they mathematically map the geometric structure of the document. The AI identifies the vertical and horizontal boundaries of the tables, correctly delineating the headers (Analyte, Result, Units, Reference Range). This allows {brandName} to accurately reconstruct the tabular data perfectly within our secure database, ensuring that a critical potassium value is never accidentally attributed to a sodium reference range.
labReports.feat1Title: The Challenge of Ingesting Tabular Data
labReports.feat1Desc: Laboratory values are the objective truth. However, their transmission as static PDFs creates a massive data entry bottleneck. {brandName} solves this with advanced computer vision.
labReports.feat2Title: Automated Abnormality Flagging and HPO Mapping
labReports.feat2Desc: Lumina automates the identification of laboratory abnormalities, mapping them directly to standardized phenotypic terms to accelerate the diagnostic process.
labReports.feat3Title: Preventing Transcription Errors and Data Silos
labReports.feat3Desc: Manual data entry of laboratory values is a persistent source of medical error. {brandName} eliminates this risk by digitizing the process directly from source records.

geneticEvidence.heroSubtitle: The ultimate confirmation in rare disease diagnosis. {brandName} supports the ingestion, structuring, and phenotypic correlation of standard commercial genetic testing reports.
geneticEvidence.overviewText: In the modern era of medical genetics, a definitive diagnosis for a rare inherited disorder almost always culminates in advanced genetic testing. Whether a physician orders a targeted multi-gene panel, Whole Exome Sequencing (WES), or chromosomal microarray analysis, the ultimate result is delivered by a highly specialized commercial or academic laboratory. However, exactly like standard clinical notes and basic laboratory values, these complex genetic results are overwhelmingly delivered back to the ordering physician as static, flattened PDF documents. These documents are notoriously dense, often spanning 20 to 30 pages filled with highly technical variant nomenclature, complex molecular interpretations, and extensive bibliographical references. {brandName}’s Natural Language Processing engines have been specifically tuned to ingest and parse these highly specialized genetic PDFs. When a laboratory report is uploaded into the patient's secure workspace, the AI systematically scans the document to extract the critical genetic entities. It identifies the specific genes implicated (e.g., FBN1, MYBPC3, COL1A1), extracts the precise molecular nomenclature of the detected variants, determines the zygosity of the findings, and crucially, extracts the clinical classification provided by the sequencing laboratory. By structuring this genetic data, {brandName} ensures that the ultimate piece of diagnostic evidence is not lost in a sea of PDF text. The extracted variants and their corresponding classifications are elevated directly to the physician's Case Dashboard, clearly displayed alongside the patient's phenotypic profile. This prevents critical genetic findings from being overlooked during rushed clinical reviews and ensures that the structured data can be easily exported via FHIR API to the hospital’s central Electronic Health Record.
geneticEvidence.feat1Desc: Complex genetic results are overwhelmingly delivered as static PDF documents. {brandName} parses these dense reports to extract genes, variants, and classifications.
geneticEvidence.feat2Title: Genotype-Phenotype Correlation and the VUS Dilemma
geneticEvidence.feat2Desc: Lumina helps physicians navigate the complexity of VUS results by correlating genetic findings with the patient's specific phenotypic presentation in real-time.
geneticEvidence.feat3Title: Strict Data Scope: Focusing on Clinical Interpretation
geneticEvidence.feat3Desc: To ensure maximum privacy, {brandName} does not ingest raw genomic sequence data. We focus on the clinical interpretation from laboratory reports.

fhirExport.heroSubtitle: Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) is the global standard for health data exchange. {brandName} speaks it fluently, ensuring seamless, bidirectional EHR synchronization.
fhirExport.overviewText: The single greatest barrier to the adoption of advanced clinical decision support software is the creation of "data silos." Historically, when a hospital purchased a specialized diagnostic tool, that tool existed in a vacuum. It required physicians to maintain separate login credentials, learn an entirely new user interface, and most egregiously, it forced administrative staff to manually copy and paste patient demographics, lab results, and final reports back and forth between the specialized tool and the hospital's primary Electronic Health Record (EHR) system, such as Epic or Cerner. This disjointed workflow is not only phenomenally inefficient, but it is also highly dangerous. Manual data entry inevitably leads to transcription errors. A specialized diagnostic report that is never properly copied back into the central EHR is effectively invisible to the rest of the patient's care team, completely defeating the purpose of the advanced diagnosis. Recognizing this critical failure point, the architects of {brandName} designed the platform from its inception to be entirely interoperable. {brandName} is not a walled garden; it is a fluid extension of your existing clinical infrastructure. We achieve this seamless integration through rigorous adherence to the HL7 Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) standard, specifically Release 4 (R4). FHIR represents the absolute bleeding edge of health data exchange, utilizing modern web APIs (RESTful architectures) to allow disparate medical software systems to communicate securely and instantaneously. By building {brandName} on a FHIR-native foundation, we guarantee that our advanced NLP engines and Bayesian scoring models operate in perfect synchronization with your hospital's central source of truth.
fhirExport.feat1Desc: Historically, specialized diagnostic tools existed in a vacuum. {brandName} breaks these silos through seamless integration with hospital EHR systems.
fhirExport.feat2Desc: Data flows securely both into and out of the {brandName} platform without requiring human intervention, ensuring bidirectional synchronization of patient records.
fhirExport.feat3Title: Security, Authentication, and the SMART on FHIR Framework
fhirExport.feat3Desc: {brandName} utilizes the modern SMART on FHIR framework for secure authentication and data structuring, following industry-best security protocols.

patientPreIntake.overviewText: By pushing data collection entirely to the digital realm before the patient arrives, {brandName} eliminates the administrative bottleneck at the front desk. referralLettersPage.heroSubtitle: Stop typing redundant clinical summaries. {brandName} instantly synthesizes approved HPO findings and demographic data into a professional, specialist-ready referral letter.
referralLettersPage.overviewText: In modern healthcare, the administrative burden placed upon physicians is staggering. After spending hours meticulously reviewing a complex rare disease case, synthesizing disjointed medical records, and finally arriving at a mathematically sound differential diagnosis, the physician’s work is still not done. They must then transition into administrative mode, spending upwards of 30 to 45 minutes manually drafting a comprehensive referral letter. This letter must accurately summarize the patient's entire history, detail the specific phenotypic findings that led to the suspected diagnosis, and justify the necessity of a highly specialized (and often expensive) genetic consultation. This redundant documentation is not just a frustrating waste of highly specialized medical talent; it actively delays patient care. Referral letters often sit in a draft state for days while the physician attends to acute clinical duties, delaying the patient's entry into the next critical phase of their diagnostic odyssey. {brandName} eliminates this administrative bottleneck entirely. By leveraging the structured data that has already been verified during the HPO review workflow, {brandName} can autonomously draft a complete, clinical-grade referral letter in less than three seconds.
referralLettersPage.feat1Desc: In modern healthcare, the administrative burden placed upon physicians is staggering. {brandName} eliminates this bottleneck through automated synthesis.
referralLettersPage.feat2Title: Automated Synthesis via Large Language Models
referralLettersPage.feat2Desc: The referral generation engine utilizes highly tuned LLMs to synthesize ground-truth data into a professional, clinical tone while strictly avoiding hallucinations.

caseTeaching.dravet-syndrome: SCN1A loss-of-function variants cause sodium channelopathy; fever-provoked prolonged hemiclonic seizures in the first year of life are a cardinal feature.
caseTeaching.marfan-syndrome: FBN1 variants disrupt fibrillin-1, causing connective tissue fragility; aortic root aneurysm with lens dislocation and skeletal features fulfils Ghent criteria.
caseTeaching.gaucher-type1: Biallelic GBA variants reduce glucocerebrosidase activity; bone marrow infiltration by lipid-laden macrophages explains cytopenias and organomegaly.
caseTeaching.phenylketonuria: PAH deficiency impairs phenylalanine hydroxylation; untreated hyperphenylalaninaemia is neurotoxic, but early dietary intervention prevents intellectual disability.
caseTeaching.turner-syndrome: 45,X monosomy causes ovarian dysgenesis and short stature; cardiac (coarctation, bicuspid aortic valve) and renal anomalies are common co-occurrences requiring surveillance.
caseTeaching.rett-syndrome: MECP2 variants cause X-linked neurodevelopmental regression almost exclusively in females; the hallmark is purposeful hand use loss with stereotypic wringing movements after 6–18 months of normal development.
caseTeaching.neurofibromatosis-type1: NF1 loss-of-function activates RAS/MAPK signalling; diagnosis requires two or more NIH criteria, and plexiform neurofibromas carry risk of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour transformation.
caseTeaching.duchenne-muscular-dystrophy: Out-of-frame DMD deletions abolish dystrophin, causing early-onset severe myopathy; creatine kinase is typically 50–100× upper limit of normal at presentation.
caseTeaching.pompe-disease: GAA variants impair lysosomal glycogen degradation; classic infantile Pompe presents with cardiomegaly and hypotonia, and enzyme replacement therapy is life-saving if started early.
caseTeaching.williams-syndrome: 7q11.23 microdeletion including ELN causes Williams syndrome; the characteristic hypersociability combined with supravalvular aortic stenosis and hypercalcaemia is pathognomonic.
caseTeaching.fabry-disease: GLA variants reduce alpha-galactosidase A activity causing lysosomal globotriaosylceramide accumulation; young males with unexplained acroparesthesia, angiokeratomas, and renal/cardiac involvement should be screened.
caseTeaching.cystic-fibrosis: Biallelic CFTR variants cause defective chloride transport; elevated sweat chloride (>60 mmol/L) remains the gold-standard diagnostic test, and CFTR modulators now offer disease-modifying treatment for many variants.
